methodologies.name: Agentic Workflow Construction
methodologies.summary: Construct an agent as four explicit components — model, tools, data stores, agent-loop prompt — and pick an interaction paradigm (passive, explicit, or autonomous) before writing the loop. Each component is named, owned, and independently swappable. The interaction paradigm sets how much latitude the model has at each turn, which determines how much guardrailing the loop must do.
methodologies.intent: Force agent authors to name the four components and the interaction paradigm before coding, so failures localise to a component rather than smearing across an undifferentiated agent.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Building a new agent from scratch — coding agent, customer-service agent, browser agent, internal-tools agent. Apply on day one before the loop is written. Don't apply when wiring up a thin LLM call with no tools or memory — that's not an agent, that's a prompt.

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the agent must accomplish, framed as a user-facing capability.

inputs.name: Available model(s)
inputs.description: Foundation model(s) accessible to the agent, with their cost / latency / capability profile.

inputs.name: Tool inventory
inputs.description: External APIs, databases, search endpoints, browsers, sandboxes the agent may use.

inputs.name: Data store(s)
inputs.description: Memory stores, vector indices, KV caches, scratchpads the agent reads and writes.

outputs.name: Named four-component agent
outputs.description: An agent whose model, tools, data stores, and loop prompt are each separately authored and swappable.

outputs.name: Declared interaction paradigm
outputs.description: One of: passive (model only generates), explicit (model uses tools when instructed), autonomous (model chooses tools and continues until done).

outputs.name: Agent loop
outputs.description: The control flow that orchestrates the four components under the chosen paradigm.

steps.name: Pick the interaction paradigm
steps.description: Passive: the model only generates text; tools/memory are invisible to it. Explicit: the loop calls tools at fixed points; the model decides content. Autonomous: the model decides what to do at each step. Pick the weakest paradigm that solves the task — autonomy is expensive and unsafe to ramp prematurely.

steps.name: Author the model component
steps.description: Choose the model, the system prompt skeleton, and the structured output format (if any). This is where capability lives; everything else is plumbing.

steps.name: Author the tool component
steps.description: Enumerate tools as typed function signatures with descriptions the model will see. Each tool returns structured data, not free prose. Validate tool returns against schemas before they re-enter the loop.

steps.name: Author the data-store component
steps.description: Pick the memory shape: scratchpad, KV store, vector index, episodic log. Decide what the model reads at each step and what the loop writes after each step.

steps.name: Author the agent-loop prompt
steps.description: Write the loop prompt that ties model, tools, and data stores together. The prompt must be explicit about which tools exist, when the loop ends, and what the success state looks like.

steps.name: Add guardrails and verifiers
steps.description: For autonomous paradigms, add input/output guardrails, tool-call validators, and a stop condition. The looser the paradigm, the more guardrailing the loop must do.

steps.name: Bind to an orchestration framework only when needed
steps.description: If the loop has fan-out, retries, observability, or state-machine shape, lift it into a framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic AI, etc.). Don't reach for orchestration on day one — a hand-rolled loop is fine for the first cycle.

principles: Name all four components explicitly — debugging an unnamed component is debugging fog.
Pick the weakest interaction paradigm that solves the task.
Tool returns are structured data, never free prose.
Guardrails grow with autonomy — passive needs almost none, autonomous needs many.

known_failure_modes.note: Adding tools without a typed schema until the model can't reliably pick the right one — failures smear across components.
Stuffing the loop prompt with every conceivable instruction until the model loses focus.
Choosing the autonomous paradigm when explicit would have sufficed, then needing heavy guardrails to compensate.

sources.title: Designing Large Language Model Applications
sources.chapter: Ch 10 (Interfacing LLMs with External Tools — Agentic Workflow Components)
sources.quote: Agentic Workflow Components (Models, Tools, Data Stores, Agent Loop Prompt)

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 1–4 (Introduction; Prompt Engineering; GPT Assistants API; Multi-agent systems)
sources.quote: Multi-agent collaboration through frameworks like AutoGen, CrewAI, and LangChain enables agents to communicate, critique, and work together on tasks.

methodologies.name: SPAR Agent Loop Design
methodologies.summary: Design the agent's operational core as a four-phase loop — Sense, Plan, Act, Reflect — making each phase an explicit, named station in the runtime. Sense ingests environment signals and task input; Plan synthesises an intended trajectory; Act executes tool calls and side-effects; Reflect compares outcome to intent and updates beliefs. The methodology insists each phase be addressable in code and observable in traces, so practitioners can diagnose where an agent is failing rather than treating it as a black box.
methodologies.intent: Give every agent a named four-phase anatomy — Sense, Plan, Act, Reflect — so behaviour, traces, and failures map cleanly onto phases instead of being smeared across an opaque loop.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply when designing the operational loop of any non-trivial agent — coding agents, research agents, browser agents, ops agents. Especially useful when teams are reaching for ReAct or autonomous loops without a clear control structure. Don't apply when building a single-shot prompt or a deterministic pipeline with no perception or reflection — there is no loop to design.

inputs.name: Environment interface
inputs.description: The sensors and observation channels the agent reads from (APIs, user turns, file system, observers).

inputs.name: Task goal
inputs.description: The objective the agent is pursuing, in a form the planner can decompose.

inputs.name: Action repertoire
inputs.description: The set of tools, effectors, or sub-agents available for the Act phase.

outputs.name: Four-phase agent loop
outputs.description: Explicit Sense, Plan, Act, Reflect stations connected by typed transitions, each independently testable and observable.

outputs.name: Phase trace schema
outputs.description: Structured trace events emitted by each phase that downstream tooling can group, search, and replay.

steps.name: Build the Sense phase
steps.description: Define what the agent observes each tick — user input, tool outputs, memory recall, environment state. Sense is responsible for assembling the situation, not interpreting it. Surface ambiguity rather than collapse it.

steps.name: Build the Plan phase
steps.description: Convert the sensed situation into an intended next move or trajectory. Use chain-of-thought, tree-of-thoughts, or explicit plan-and-execute when the task warrants it. Output is a plan artefact, not a free monologue.

steps.name: Build the Act phase
steps.description: Execute the plan via tool calls, sub-agent dispatches, or external effectors. Act is the only phase allowed to cause side-effects; Sense, Plan, and Reflect remain read-only.

steps.name: Build the Reflect phase
steps.description: Compare the outcome of Act against the plan's intent. Update beliefs, log lessons, decide whether to loop, replan, escalate, or stop. Reflect is where the agent earns its 'agentic' label.

steps.name: Wire phase transitions and termination
steps.description: Define typed transitions (Sense→Plan→Act→Reflect→Sense or →stop). Add a step budget and explicit stop conditions so the loop is bounded.

steps.name: Instrument each phase as a first-class trace event
steps.description: Emit structured trace events tagged with the phase. Downstream observability tools group, filter, and replay by phase so failures localise.

principles: Every agent has the same four-phase anatomy — make it visible, don't hide it.
Only the Act phase causes side-effects; the other three are observation, deliberation, and learning.
Reflect is non-optional — an agent that doesn't reflect is a scripted pipeline.
Phase boundaries are testable contracts, not narrative flourishes.

known_failure_modes.note: Loop runs Sense→Plan→Act→Reflect→Sense forever because Reflect lacks a stop condition or step budget.
Reflect uses the same model and prompt as Plan, so the agent rubber-stamps its own decisions.
Phases share mutable state outside their typed transitions, breaking the localisation guarantee SPAR is supposed to give.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence
sources.chapter: Ch 4 'Inside the Mind of an AI Agent' (pp. 83–107)
sources.quote: The SPAR framework — Sense, Plan, Act, Reflect (SPAR) — is introduced as the core behavior pattern of effective AI agents.

methodologies.name: BDI Agent Construction Methodology
methodologies.summary: Construct an agent as a triple of belief base, desire (goal) set, and intention stack, driven by a deliberation cycle that selects plans from a plan library to achieve desires. The methodology operationalises agent-oriented programming as understood in the Jason/JaCaMo lineage: beliefs are first-class data, plans are first-class code, and the runtime cycle is a publicly visible artefact. Especially valuable when LLM-only agents lack the introspectability and goal-tracking that BDI provides natively.
methodologies.intent: Produce agents whose internal state — beliefs, desires, intentions — is explicitly represented and inspectable, so behaviour is explainable and verifiable rather than emergent.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Reach for BDI when an agent must reason explicitly about goals, commit to long-running intentions, coordinate with other agents under social rules, or be auditable to a regulator. Particularly suited to multi-agent organisations on platforms like Jason, JaCaMo, or Jadex. Don't apply when the task is one-shot generation, a thin tool-using chatbot, or when no goal persists beyond a single turn — BDI's machinery is overhead for stateless work. Skip when the team has no appetite for symbolic agent programming.

inputs.name: Domain ontology
inputs.description: Vocabulary of predicates the agent will believe — facts about the world, other agents, and itself.

inputs.name: Goal catalogue
inputs.description: The desires the agent may adopt, with priorities and adoption conditions.

inputs.name: Plan library
inputs.description: Plans indexed by triggering event and context — the agent's procedural knowledge.

outputs.name: BDI agent program
outputs.description: An agent definition (e.g. in AgentSpeak/Jason) with belief base, goals, and plan library.

outputs.name: Deliberation cycle configuration
outputs.description: Selection functions for event, plan, and intention selection, plus the reasoning-cycle parameters.

steps.name: Model the belief base
steps.description: Define the predicates the agent will hold about the world, including beliefs about its own internal state and about other agents. Decide which beliefs are perceptual (refreshed each cycle) and which are persistent.

steps.name: Enumerate desires and adoption conditions
steps.description: List the goals the agent may pursue. Each desire has a triggering condition and a priority. Distinguish achievement goals from maintenance goals.

steps.name: Author the plan library
steps.description: Write plans as triggering-event + context + body. The body is a sequence of belief updates, sub-goals, and external actions. Plans are the unit of procedural knowledge — keep them small and composable.

steps.name: Define selection functions
steps.description: Specify how the agent selects events, plans, and intentions when multiple are applicable. Defaults exist in Jason/JaCaMo but should be overridden for non-trivial domains.

steps.name: Wire the deliberation cycle
steps.description: Configure the perceive-deliberate-act cycle: perceive environment, update beliefs, select events, choose plans, execute intentions. Tune cycle rate for the domain.

steps.name: Integrate with environment and organisation
steps.description: On JaCaMo, attach the agent to an environment (CArtAgO artefacts) and a social organisation (MOISE roles, groups, schemes). This is where multi-agent coordination is grounded.

principles: Beliefs, desires, and intentions are first-class artefacts — readable, inspectable, debuggable.
Plans are libraries, not improvisations — composition over emergence.
Commitment matters — an intention persists until it is achieved, dropped, or impossible.
Environment and organisation are programmed alongside the agent, not assumed.

known_failure_modes.note: Plan-selection logic loops between two equally applicable plans without committing — the agent deliberates forever.
Plans mutate shared beliefs as side-effects, breaking the explicit BDI contract.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition)
sources.quote: Agent-Oriented Programming, Agent Languages, Organization and Environment Programming, JaCaMo Platform

methodologies.name: Behavior Tree Back-Chaining Construction
methodologies.summary: Construct an agentic behavior tree by starting from the goal and recursively adding actions whose post-conditions satisfy the parent node's preconditions, until every leaf is directly executable. Behavior trees provide structured, modular control flow; back-chaining ensures the tree is constructed by purpose, not by ad-hoc authoring. Leaves combine LLM calls and tool invocations, and the tree itself becomes the audit trail of how the agent's behaviour is composed.
methodologies.intent: Produce an agentic behavior tree whose every node exists because it contributes to satisfying the parent's preconditions, eliminating dead branches and ad-hoc structure.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply when designing structured agent control flow with mixed LLM and tool nodes — game-like environments, multi-step task agents, complex workflows. Especially useful when prompt-only agents have become unmanageable spaghetti. Don't apply to simple linear pipelines or single-shot prompts — a behavior tree is overhead. Skip when the task is genuinely open-ended exploration with no goal decomposition possible.

inputs.name: Goal specification
inputs.description: The terminal condition the agent must achieve, expressed as a checkable predicate.

inputs.name: Action inventory
inputs.description: Candidate actions (LLM prompts, tool calls, sub-agents) annotated with preconditions and post-conditions.

outputs.name: Agentic behavior tree
outputs.description: An executable tree whose root is the goal and whose leaves are concrete LLM-or-tool actions, every node justified by back-chaining.

outputs.name: Pre/post-condition map
outputs.description: For each node, the conditions under which it runs and what it asserts after running — the contract the tree was built against.

steps.name: State the goal as a root precondition
steps.description: Express the agent's terminal goal as a predicate to be satisfied. This becomes the precondition of the root node.

steps.name: Find actions whose post-conditions satisfy the parent
steps.description: Search the action inventory for actions whose post-conditions imply the parent's precondition. Each candidate becomes a child node.

steps.name: Recurse on each child's preconditions
steps.description: Treat each child's preconditions as new sub-goals and repeat. Terminate recursion when an action's preconditions are trivially satisfied (initial state) or directly observable.

steps.name: Choose composite node types
steps.description: For each non-leaf, choose sequence, fallback (selector), or parallel composition based on whether children's effects are conjunctive, alternative, or independent.

steps.name: Wire leaves to LLM calls and tool invocations
steps.description: Implement leaf nodes as ReAct-style LLM calls, tool invocations, or sub-agents. Each leaf returns success/failure/running so the tree composes correctly.

steps.name: Validate the tree end-to-end
steps.description: Walk the tree from leaves upward checking that every node's children's combined post-conditions satisfy its precondition. Trim or repair branches that don't.

principles: Every node exists because it satisfies its parent's precondition — no ad-hoc nodes.
Pre- and post-conditions are first-class contracts, not comments.
Composite node choice is deliberate: sequence for conjunction, fallback for alternative, parallel for independence.
Leaves combine LLM and tool calls — behavior trees are not LLM-only.

known_failure_modes.note: Tree treats a partially-true post-condition as fully satisfied and skips a needed branch.
Each leaf adds a new tool without consolidation, so the leaf layer becomes unmanageable even though the tree above it is clean.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.quote: 6.5 Building ABTs with back chaining ... Agentic Behavior Trees (ABTs) sequence a series of nodes that consist of LLM calls and tool use

methodologies.name: Four-Tier Agent Memory Construction
methodologies.summary: Build agent memory as four cooperating tiers — conversational (turn-window context), semantic (facts and embeddings), episodic (past interaction traces), and procedural (learned skills and routines). Each tier has its own write policy, retrieval mechanism, and compression strategy. The methodology rejects the single-vector-store-as-memory shortcut and forces the team to decide, per tier, what is stored, how it is retrieved, and how it is compressed when capacity is exceeded.
methodologies.intent: Replace 'agent memory = one vector store' with an explicit four-tier architecture that distinguishes conversational, semantic, episodic, and procedural memory, each with its own policies.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply when designing memory for any agent that must retain context beyond a single conversation — assistants, coding agents, long-running ops agents. Especially valuable when teams have hit recall problems with a naive single-vector store. Don't apply to single-turn agents or stateless tools. Skip when the agent's memory needs are fully covered by the model's context window and there is no need for retrieval at all.

inputs.name: Memory budget
inputs.description: Token, storage, and latency budgets for each tier.

inputs.name: Recall scenarios
inputs.description: The questions or situations where the agent must remember something — what tier each one should be served from.

outputs.name: Four-tier memory architecture
outputs.description: Conversational, semantic, episodic, and procedural stores with explicit interfaces.

outputs.name: Per-tier policies
outputs.description: Write, retrieval, and compression policies for each tier, documented and tuneable.

steps.name: Build the conversational tier
steps.description: Window the live conversation context. Decide truncation policy (sliding, summary-augmented, hierarchical) and what gets promoted to longer-lived tiers.

steps.name: Build the semantic tier
steps.description: Store extracted facts and embeddings keyed for similarity retrieval. Decide what is extractable as a fact and what stays in episodic.

steps.name: Build the episodic tier
steps.description: Persist concrete past interactions or events with timestamps and identifiers. Episodes are the agent's autobiography — used for recall of 'what happened last time'.

steps.name: Build the procedural tier
steps.description: Store skills, routines, and successful plans the agent can replay. This is how an agent learns to be faster or better at a recurring task.

steps.name: Define retrieval orchestration across tiers
steps.description: Decide which tier is queried for which kind of recall request, and how their results are merged or prioritised.

steps.name: Define compression and forgetting policies
steps.description: Each tier needs a compression strategy: summarisation, clustering, recency-weighted decay, forced eviction. Without explicit policies the tiers grow unboundedly.

principles: Memory is not one store — it is four cooperating systems with different jobs.
Every tier has explicit write, read, and compression policies.
Retrieval is orchestrated across tiers, not delegated to a single vector index.
Forgetting is a feature — design it on purpose.

known_failure_modes.note: Without per-tier write policies, attacker-controlled content can be promoted from conversational to semantic or procedural memory.
Tiers retrieve disconnected fragments that recombine into an incoherent context — symptom of missing retrieval orchestration.
Notes the agent wrote to itself in conversational memory are later retrieved as if they were source evidence — a confusion that crisp tier boundaries prevent.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 8 §8.6 'Implementing memory in agentic systems', §8.7 'Understanding memory and knowledge compression'
sources.quote: AI agents utilize conversational, semantic, episodic, and procedural memory, with compression techniques improving retrieval efficiency and relevance

methodologies.name: Plan-Reason-Evaluate-Feedback Loop
methodologies.summary: Construct the agent's control logic as a four-stage loop: Plan generates a candidate trajectory, Reason elaborates the trajectory using chain-of-thought or tree-of-thoughts, Evaluate scores the result with self-consistency or a judge, and Feedback returns lessons to the planner for the next iteration. The methodology insists each stage be a distinct, instrumented station so the team can tune them independently — instead of a single mega-prompt doing all four jobs poorly.
methodologies.intent: Decompose the agent's control loop into Plan, Reason, Evaluate, and Feedback stages so each can be authored, tested, and tuned independently rather than collapsed into one prompt.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply when the agent must take on tasks complex enough to require explicit planning and self-evaluation — research agents, coding agents, multi-step problem solvers. Especially useful when a monolithic ReAct prompt has plateaued in quality. Don't apply to single-shot generators or simple tool callers — adding four stages to a trivial task is gold-plating. Skip when latency budgets cannot absorb the extra round-trips.

inputs.name: Task specification
inputs.description: What the agent must accomplish, decomposable into sub-steps.

inputs.name: Evaluator definition
inputs.description: The judge, rubric, or self-consistency mechanism that will score Reason outputs.

outputs.name: Four-stage control loop
outputs.description: Plan, Reason, Evaluate, Feedback as distinct, instrumented stages.

outputs.name: Stage-level metrics
outputs.description: Per-stage success/failure signals that downstream telemetry can graph and alert on.

steps.name: Author the Plan stage
steps.description: Generate a candidate plan or trajectory. Use single-path or multi-path plan generation depending on cost and risk.

steps.name: Author the Reason stage
steps.description: Elaborate the plan with chain-of-thought or tree-of-thoughts. Reasoning is generative; do not collapse it into Plan.

steps.name: Author the Evaluate stage
steps.description: Score the reasoning output. Use self-consistency, an LLM judge, or a rubric. Evaluate must be independent enough to dissent.

steps.name: Author the Feedback stage
steps.description: Convert evaluation into actionable signal for the planner — accept, reject with reasons, replan, or escalate. Feedback closes the loop.

steps.name: Instrument each stage independently
steps.description: Emit stage-scoped traces and metrics. Tuning is impossible if the four stages share telemetry.

steps.name: Bound iterations
steps.description: Add a max-iterations budget and a convergence test so the loop terminates rather than oscillating between plan and reflection forever.

principles: Plan, Reason, Evaluate, and Feedback are four jobs — give each its own prompt and metric.
The evaluator must be able to dissent — same-model self-critique is a failure mode, not a method.
Feedback closes the loop or it isn't feedback.
Iteration is bounded — set a budget and a convergence test.

known_failure_modes.note: Evaluate uses the same model and context as Reason and rubber-stamps every result.
Feedback keeps reopening the plan without convergence; missing iteration budget or convergence test.
Feedback returns to Plan unboundedly because no stage has authority to declare done.

sources.title: AI Agents in Action
sources.chapter: Ch 11 §11.5 'Applying planning, reasoning, evaluation, and feedback to assistant and agentic systems'
sources.quote: 11.5.1 Application of assistant/agentic planning, 11.5.2 Application of assistant/agentic reasoning, 11.5.3 Application of evaluation to agentic systems, 11.5.4 Application of feedback to assistant/agentic applications

methodologies.name: Agent Count Escalation
methodologies.summary: Start with one agent. Add a second only when the first measurably fails the task, and pick the multi-agent coordination style — democratic, manager, hierarchical, actor-critic — deliberately rather than reaching for a swarm by reflex. The methodology treats agent count as a tuning dial that should be moved only under evidence, with each escalation paired with a coordination shape that fits the failure mode.
methodologies.intent: Make 'how many agents' an evidence-driven decision and force a deliberate choice of coordination style at each escalation, instead of jumping to multi-agent by reflex.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply when teams are tempted to design a multi-agent system before a single-agent baseline exists, or when a single agent is plateauing and the next move is unclear. Don't apply when the task genuinely is multi-actor by nature (negotiation, simulation, debate as feature) — there the multi-agent shape is part of the requirement. Skip when there is no measurement of single-agent failure on which to base an escalation.

inputs.name: Single-agent baseline metrics
inputs.description: Quantified evidence of where the single agent succeeds and fails.

inputs.name: Coordination cost budget
inputs.description: Latency, token, and infra budget available for adding more agents.

outputs.name: Agent-count decision
outputs.description: Documented choice of 1, few, or many agents with the evidence that justifies it.

outputs.name: Coordination shape
outputs.description: Named coordination style (democratic, manager, hierarchical, actor-critic, swarm) with explicit roles.

steps.name: Build and measure the single-agent baseline
steps.description: Implement the simplest single agent that could plausibly solve the task. Measure success, latency, and failure modes against a representative eval set.

steps.name: Identify the specific failure that escalation should fix
steps.description: Name the failure mode the second agent will address — context overflow, role conflict, missing specialist skill, evaluator independence. Without a named failure, escalation is theatre.

steps.name: Pick the coordination style
steps.description: Choose deliberately: democratic (peers vote), manager (one orchestrator with workers), hierarchical (nested supervisors), actor-critic (one generates, one judges). Each fits a different failure mode.

steps.name: Add the smallest number of agents that resolves the failure
steps.description: Two is often enough. Resist the temptation to leap to a swarm — extra agents add coordination cost and new failure modes.

steps.name: Re-measure against the same eval set
steps.description: Compare the multi-agent system to the single-agent baseline on the same metrics. If gains are marginal, roll back.

steps.name: Document the escalation as a decision record
steps.description: Capture why the escalation happened, what failure it fixed, and the conditions under which de-escalation is appropriate. Future teams need this.

principles: Single agent first — multi-agent is a tuning move, not a starting point.
Every escalation is paired with a named failure it is fixing.
Coordination style is a deliberate choice from a small menu, not 'whatever the framework defaults to'.
Re-measure after escalating; roll back if gains are marginal.

known_failure_modes.note: Single agent is overloaded with responsibilities the team refuses to split, even after the failure mode is named — escalation needed but not taken.
Escalation went to 'as many agents as needed' instead of a bounded coordination shape — coordination cost explodes.
Chained agents lose information at each handoff because no coordination style was deliberately chosen.
Manager-style coordination was picked without checking whether the orchestrator becomes the new single point of failure.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: How Many Agents, Single-Agent and Multiagent Scenarios, Swarms, Principles for Adding Agents, Multiagent Coordination (Democratic, Manager, Hierarchical, Actor-Critic)

methodologies.name: MAESTRO Threat Modeling
methodologies.summary: Run an agent-specific threat-modeling pass before deployment using MAESTRO categories: foundation-model risks, data-protection threats, internal/external attack vectors, and red-team adversarial probes. The methodology adapts classical threat modeling (STRIDE, LINDDUN) to the unique attack surface of agentic systems — prompt injection, memory poisoning, tool misuse, exfiltration, agent-on-agent attacks — and produces a defensive-control catalogue tied to each identified threat.
methodologies.intent: Replace generic security review with an agent-aware threat-modeling discipline that enumerates agent-specific attack categories and pairs each with a concrete defensive control before deployment.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply before any agent goes to production, especially when it has tool access, autonomous loops, multi-agent coordination, or handles sensitive data. Use again after every material capability change. Don't apply as a substitute for application-level AppSec — MAESTRO complements, it doesn't replace. Skip when the system is genuinely a single-turn chat with no tools, no memory, and no access to private data — there is no agentic attack surface to model.

inputs.name: Agent architecture
inputs.description: Model, tools, memory, loop, and integrations — all of which form the attack surface.

inputs.name: Data classification
inputs.description: What data the agent reads, writes, or transmits, and its sensitivity.

inputs.name: Adversary model
inputs.description: Who would attack this agent and what they want — external attacker, malicious user, compromised tool, hostile agent.

outputs.name: Threat catalogue
outputs.description: Enumerated threats grouped by MAESTRO category with severity and likelihood.

outputs.name: Defensive control map
outputs.description: For each high-priority threat, the specific control (pattern, guardrail, isolation boundary) deployed to mitigate it.

outputs.name: Red-team report
outputs.description: Findings from adversarial probing of the agent against the catalogued threats.

steps.name: Enumerate foundation-model threats
steps.description: Catalogue risks rooted in the model itself: jailbreaks, prompt injection, hallucination, sycophancy, data extraction from training. Decide which require mitigation.

steps.name: Enumerate data-protection threats
steps.description: Identify where sensitive data enters, lives in, or leaves the agent. Trace PII, secrets, and proprietary content across context, memory, tool calls, and outputs.

steps.name: Enumerate internal threats
steps.description: Threats from within the agent system: memory poisoning, agent scheming, hidden mode switching, role drift, agent-on-agent attacks.

steps.name: Enumerate external threat vectors
steps.description: Threats from outside: supply-chain compromise of tools or models, hostile tool outputs, malicious documents in retrieval corpora, network-level attacks.

steps.name: Map defensive controls to each threat
steps.description: For each high-priority threat, name the specific control: input filtering, output filtering, sandbox, dual-LLM, allowlist, human-in-the-loop, policy gate.

steps.name: Red-team the agent against the catalogue
steps.description: Probe the deployed agent with adversarial prompts, malicious tool outputs, and crafted memory injections. Document where controls held and where they failed.

principles: Threat modeling for agents is not generic AppSec — it must enumerate agent-specific categories.
Every catalogued threat is paired with a concrete control or an explicit accepted-risk note.
Red-team probing is part of the methodology, not optional follow-up.
Re-run the pass after every material capability change — new tools, new data, new autonomy.

known_failure_modes.note: Memory threat was acknowledged in the catalogue but not paired with an authentication or quorum control.
Tool-access threat was modelled but no policy-gating control was added before deployment.
External-threat enumeration missed the tool-provider supply chain.
Tool outputs treated as trustworthy because no defensive control was tied to that specific threat.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: Unique Risks, Emerging Threat Vectors, Securing Foundation Models, Defensive Techniques, Red Teaming, Threat Modeling with MAESTRO, Data Protection

methodologies.name: Agent Architecture Decision Ladder
methodologies.summary: Choose the agent architecture on a four-rung ladder: plain model call, single agent, deterministic multi-agent workflow, autonomous multi-agent system. Pick the least powerful rung that solves the task. The methodology forces a defensible choice rather than reflexive escalation to autonomous MAS, treating architecture as a cost-benefit decision with explicit criteria at each rung.
methodologies.intent: Make architecture choice a deliberate, evidence-bound climb up a four-rung ladder — picking the least powerful rung that solves the task — instead of defaulting to autonomous multi-agent systems.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Apply at project kickoff before any architecture is implemented, and at every major capability addition that might trigger an escalation. Don't apply when the architecture is externally constrained (a vendor mandate or an existing framework choice already locks the rung). Skip when the team has no measurement discipline — without evals to compare rungs, the ladder is just opinion.

inputs.name: Task profile
inputs.description: Whether the task is text generation, action-taking, structured collaboration, or open-ended exploration.

inputs.name: Determinism requirement
inputs.description: How much variability stakeholders accept in agent behaviour and outputs.

inputs.name: Cost and latency budget
inputs.description: Per-task tokens, infra spend, and end-to-end latency limits.

outputs.name: Architecture decision
outputs.description: One of: plain model, single agent, deterministic MAS workflow, autonomous MAS — with the reasoning recorded.

outputs.name: Escalation triggers
outputs.description: Named conditions under which to climb to the next rung in the future.

steps.name: Profile the task on the ladder dimensions
steps.description: Score the task on action-taking need, decomposability, determinism requirement, and exploration vs. exploitation. These dimensions decide the rung.

steps.name: Rung 1 — plain model call
steps.description: If the task is text generation with no tools, no memory, and no multi-step planning, stop at the model. Wrap it in an API and ship.

steps.name: Rung 2 — single agent
steps.description: If the task requires tool use, memory, or a loop, climb to a single agent with the four-component architecture. Most production agents stop here.

steps.name: Rung 3 — deterministic multi-agent workflow
steps.description: If the task is decomposable into a fixed graph of specialist steps, build a deterministic multi-agent workflow with orchestrator-workers or pipes-and-filters. Structure is in the graph, not in the agents.

steps.name: Rung 4 — autonomous multi-agent system
steps.description: Only when the task is genuinely open-ended, requires emergent coordination, or benefits from agent-to-agent negotiation. Pay the cost of nondeterminism and coordination overhead deliberately.

steps.name: Document the decision and escalation triggers
steps.description: Record which rung was chosen and why. Name the conditions that would justify climbing higher later — so the next escalation is evidence-bound, not vibes.

principles: Pick the least powerful rung that solves the task.
Architecture choice is a written decision with evidence, not a reflex.
Each rung has a defining capability and a defining cost — know both before climbing.
Escalation triggers are named in advance — climb on evidence, not enthusiasm.

known_failure_modes.note: Stuck on Rung 2 with one over-stuffed agent because the team won't climb to Rung 3 even when the task is decomposable.
Jumped to Rung 4 without escalation triggers, ending up with an autonomous MAS for a task that Rung 3 would have solved deterministically.
Picked the rung that demoed well rather than the rung the production task needs.
Treated rung choice as 'always go higher' the same way teams treat model choice as 'always pick the biggest'.

sources.title: Designing Multi-Agent Systems
sources.chapter: Ch 1 §1.7 'Choosing the Right AI Agent Architecture', §1.7.1 'Decision Framework'
sources.quote: Building AI applications requires choosing the right level of complexity from a spectrum of architectures: simple models for text generation, single agents for action-taking, multi-agent workflows for structured deterministic collaboration, and autonomous multi-agent systems for exploratory problem-solving
